title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
How Is Your Term Insurance Impacted If You Are in a Coma?
A term insurance policy pays a benefit if the insured dies during the policy tenure. Even if you suffer a major illness which results in death, the term plan pays the promised death benefit. But what happens in the case of a coma? Is the term insurance coverage available?
Term insurance cover for Coma
A comatose state is defined as an unconscious state that continues for a prolonged period. It can result from an accidental injury, illness, stroke, tumour in the brain, etc.
In a coma, the individual is not considered dead. Even if the coma continues for months, the patient is said to be in a vegetative state, not technically dead.
Since there is no death, the term insurance cover does not pay any benefit.
The financial repercussions of a coma
Falling into a coma has far-reaching financial repercussions for the family. First, the considerable hospital bills keep piling up for each day the individual is in a comatose state. Secondly, if the individual is the sole breadwinner, the family loses its source of income. If the individual has a business, handling the business to generate revenue becomes challenging. In the case of a job, though the salary for the initial few days might be paid, in prolonged cases, the salary income might also stop. Lastly, the associated medical expenses of the comatose patient are also an added financial burden for the family.
Overall, the family might become financially crippled if the breadwinner suffers an accident or injury and falls into a coma.
How to deal with the financial repercussions of a coma?
While the term plan does not pay the death benefit in a coma, there’s a way to secure financial assistance in such an emergency. The solution is a rider.
Riders are additional coverage benefits that are available with life insurance plans and term plans included. These riders cover specific contingencies and pay a benefit if the insured event happens. For instance, an accidental death benefit rider covers accidental deaths and pays a benefit in such situations.
When dealing with a coma's financial repercussions, a critical illness rider comes into the picture. When added to the term plan, the rider can pay a benefit even in a coma when the term plan does not pay any benefit. You can get an accurate premium with the help of a term insurance calculator.
Term insurance with critical illness rider for covering coma
Almost all term insurance plans allow the option of a critical illness rider. The rider covers a list of critical illnesses and surgical procedures, and a coma is usually included in the list. You can opt for the rider when buying the term plan or at the time of renewals. In some term plans, the rider might also come as an inbuilt benefit that helps in a coma.
The rider coverage has an independent sum insured. If the insured suffers from a coma, the rider sum insured is paid in a lump sum.
Benefits of the critical illness rider:
Here are the benefits that the critical illness rider offers –
Financial assistance to the family:
The primary benefit of the rider is the financial assistance that it gives to the family if the insured suffers a coma. The rider benefit is paid in a lump sum which comes to the family’s aid. The family can tackle their financial needs easily.
Multipurpose use:
The rider benefit has multipurpose use. You can use the benefit for advanced treatments, paying hospital bills, taking care of your family’s financial needs, and even paying out outstanding loans. This makes the rider benefit flexible and allows the family to use it at their discretion.
Access to quality healthcare:
When you or your family get the rider benefit, affording advanced treatments at the ideal hospitals becomes possible. You or your loved ones can access quality healthcare facilities to get the ideal possible chance at recovery. You can calculate the premium of the term plan with your critical illness rider with the help of a term insurance calculator.
Tax benefits:
Adding the critical illness rider to the term insurance plan is also tax-saving in nature. You can claim an additional deduction on the rider premium under Section 80D1 of the Income Tax Act, 1961. So, with the rider, you not only get additional protection but additional tax savings too.
Conclusion
A coma is an unexpected and serious medical emergency. It causes not only considerable physiological trauma but financial trauma too. While physiological trauma is ideally handled by medical experts, you can prepare to avoid the associated financial trauma. Opt for term insurance with critical illness rider so that even if the term plan does not pay any benefit, the critical illness rider does. This financial benefit would help your family deal with the sky-high expenses of treatments as well as the financial needs that they might have.
